topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The key felt cold and heavy in Elias’s palm, a leaden weight that settled deep in his chest. Grandma Elara’s house, sprawling and shadowed at the edge of the woods, was suddenly his. He kept glancing at the windows, the glass reflecting back a distorted image of the overgrown gardens. Every creak of the old floorboards sounded like a deliberate footstep just beyond the periphery of his vision. He checked the deadbolt three times, his knuckles white against the brass. The silence of the inherited property was oppressive, thick enough to choke on. He started taping small, nearly invisible strips of silver foil along the window frames, a futile attempt to seal out whatever unseen presence lingered in the dust motes dancing in the slivers of light. He couldn't sleep; the shadows in the corners seemed to shift when he blinked.

[story 2]

“Are you sure this is safe, Maya?” Leo asked, his voice tight, his gaze constantly flicking toward the darkened hallway. He ran a hand over the rough texture of the antique wallpaper, the pattern seeming to writhe under his touch. He’d woken up in the master bedroom, and the air felt strangely still, devoid of natural sound. He’d heard a whisper, faint as dry leaves skittering across pavement, but when he turned, the room was empty, save for the deep, inky blackness pooling beneath the bed. Leo moved quickly, his breath catching in his throat, his eyes darting to the door as if expecting something to materialize from the threshold. He began systematically checking every lock, every latch, his movements jerky and too fast. The house felt like it was breathing, and he was certain it was holding its breath just for him.

[story 3]

I traced the outline of the portrait in the drawing-room, my reflection in the glass looking strangely hollowed out. The inheritance had come with a peculiar chill, a pervasive sense that someone was watching from the periphery of my sight. I kept rearranging the heavy, ornate furniture, nudging a chair just so, hoping to disrupt some unseen pattern. Every time I heard the distant sound of a car passing on the main road, I froze, my muscles tensing beneath my skin. It wasn't just the isolation; it was the feeling of being observed, cataloged, judged by the very architecture of the place. I started leaving small offerings—a perfectly polished stone on the mantelpiece, a single, unbroken feather on the windowsill—just to see if the atmosphere would change, if the scrutiny would lessen. But the stillness only deepened, becoming predatory.
